coredata.dc:title: Immunosensing of breast cancer tumor protein CA 15-3 (carbohydrate antigen 15.3) using a novel nano-bioink: A new platform for screening of proteins in human biofluids by pen-on-paper technology
coredata.dc:creator: Saadati, Arezoo
Hassanpour, Soodabeh
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Shadjou, Nasrin
Hassanzadeh, Ahmad
coredata.dc:description: Early stage diagnosis of breast cancer by monitoring the proteins in human biological fluids is the best method and the first section toward efficient therapy, delaying metastasis and hindrance mortality. In this study, graphite ink was synthesized and combined with CA 15-3 antibody in order to develop a novel immunosensor for detection of CA 15-3, breast cancer biomarker. Conductivity of bioink was examined by designing various conductive patterns on paper using a rollerball pen. Electrochemical behavior of engineered immunosensor was evaluated through employing sensitive diagnostic technique, DPV (differential pulse voltammetry). Under optimal conditions, the linear concentration range and low limit of quantification (LLOQ) of designed immunosensor was 15–250 U/mL and 15 U/mL, respectively. The process was successfully applied to assay of breast cancer biomarker (CA15-3) in unprocessed human plasma specimens.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Protein
Immunoassay
Biomarker
Breast cancer
Point-of-care
Biomedical analysis
Nanotechnology
Nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphite ink and CA 15-3 conjugated graphite ink (bioink)
The FE-SEM characterization of graphite ink and bioink
Fabrication of bioink on the surface of GCE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographic images of various patterns with different thicknesses and shapes obtained by graphite ink on photo paper.

(A to C) FE-SEM images of the bulk ink, (D to F) FE-SEM images of the bulk bioink, (G to I) FE-SEM images of the ink on the surface of GCE, and (J to M) FE-SEM images of the bioink on the surface of GCE in different magnifications.
EDS spectra of (A) the bulk bioink, (B) the ink modified GCE, (C) the bioink modified GCE.

A to D) CVs, DPVs, SWVs, and ChA, respectively in the potential range of −1 to +1 V at scan rate = 100 mV/s for GCE, GCE/graphite ink, GCE/bioink, GCE/bioink/BSA, GCE/bioink/BSA/Ag. E = 0.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl and the duration time was 500 s for ChA. Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. E and F) Histogram of the peak currents obtained by CV and DPV, respectively in different steps of electrode preparation.
CVs of a GCE/bioink in the presence 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− with different potential sweep rates (20–200 mV/s).

(A) Dependency of oxidation peak potentials (Epa) versus Napierian logarithm of scan rates using CVs of GCE/bioink. (B) The plot of Napierian logarithm of oxidation peak currents (ln Ipa) versus Napierian logarithm of scan rates (ln V) using GCE/bioink electrode. (C) Variation of oxidation peak currents versus square root of scan rate.

A) DPVs obtained from bioink/BSA/GCE for analysis of different concentrations of CA15–3 antigen (15, 50, 125 and 250 U/mL) in supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. Step potential = 0.2 V, E pulse = 0.05 V, t pulse = 0.2 s. Scan rate = 100 mV/s. B) Calibration curve of bioink/BSA/ CA15–3 antigen/GC electrode.

A) DPVs obtained from bioink/BSA/GCE for analysis of different concentrations of CA15–3 antigen (3.75, 15, 125 and 250 U/mL) in human plasma sample. Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. Step potential = 0.2 V, E pulse = 0.05 V, t pulse = 0.2 s. Scan rate = 100 mV/s. B) Calibration curve of bioink/BSA/ CA15–3 antigen/GC electrode.
A schematic representation of ink and bioink synthesis procedure.
The preparation and operation of engineered immunosensor for detection of CA 15.3 based on bioink.
Analysis of different conductive patterns resistance with ohmmeter.
A comparison of the performance of several immunosensor for the detection of CA15-3.

head.ce:title: Immunosensing of breast cancer tumor protein CA 15-3 (carbohydrate antigen 15.3) using a novel nano-bioink: A new platform for screening of proteins in human biofluids by pen-on-paper technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nano Technology, Faculty of Science, Urmia University, Urmia 57154, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Processing, Helmholtz-Institute Freiberg for Resource Technology, Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf, Chemnitzer Str. 40, 09599 Freiberg, Germany

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Early stage diagnosis of breast cancer by monitoring the proteins in human biological fluids is the best method and the first section toward efficient therapy, delaying metastasis and hindrance mortality. In this study, graphite ink was synthesized and combined with CA 15-3 antibody in order to develop a novel immunosensor for detection of CA 15-3, breast cancer biomarker. Conductivity of bioink was examined by designing various conductive patterns on paper using a rollerball pen. Electrochemical behavior of engineered immunosensor was evaluated through employing sensitive diagnostic technique, DPV (differential pulse voltammetry). Under optimal conditions, the linear concentration range and low limit of quantification (LLOQ) of designed immunosensor was 15–250 U/mL and 15 U/mL, respectively. The process was successfully applied to assay of breast cancer biomarker (CA15-3) in unprocessed human plasma specimens.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is the most prevalent cancer in women all over the world and is the foremost reason of cancer associated mortality. Various tumor biomarkers, both serum-based and tissue, are recently employed in the monitoring of breast cancer stages. The most broadly utilized serum marker is CA 15-3 [1]. Carbohydrate antigen 15-3 (CA 15-3) is one of the most pivotal breast cancer biomarker which plays crucial role in screening of breast cancer [2–4]. Carbohydrate antigen 15-3 is especially valuable for controlling treatment in patients whom radiological actions cannot be employed for evaluation [1]. Hence, development of completely safe analytical procedures for sensitive and selective detection of CA 15-3 biomarker at extremely low concentrations in physiological environments and biological fluids is highly demanded.

Some analytical methods have been improved for detection of CA 15-3, including; chemiluminescence immunoassay [5], quartz crystal microbalance [6], enzyme-linked immunosorption assay [7], optical immunoassay [8] and sandwich-type immunosensor [9]. All of these traditional methods requires laborious procedures, long analysis time and costly equipment. But, immunosensors have the cardinal virtue of high sensitivity, rapidity in response, costing low as well as specificity in determination, so they have been widely applied in the detection of tumor biomarkers [10–18].

Printed electronics establish an emerging category of materials which have potential to apply in sensors, batteries, transistors, photovoltaics, antennas and displays. Recently, paper substrates have drawn a lot of attention because of being low-cost, and providing platform for lightweight, disposable and flexible devices. As devices need conductive electrodes which have been deposited by airbrush spraying, sputter coating and inkjet printing. Although, these deposition techniques are either expensive or use dilute inks that quickly penetrate the paper substrate. Hence, pen-on-paper strategy employed for creation of flexible printed electronics in which conductive inks filled in rollerball pen and used for direct writing of conductive texts, electronic art, interconnect for 3D (three-dimensional) antennas and LED (light emitting diode) arrays on paper [19–21]. As a flexible substrate, paper has attracted attention in the microfluidics and miniaturization community because of its biodegradability, low-cost, porosity, biocompatibility and passive fluid transport through capillary performance. Therefore, paper substrates provide numerous plus points for devices based on printed electronic [22].

The PoP (pen-on-paper) model provides a unique technique for fabrication of flexible and versatile bio-devices through utilizing a patterning tool which is itself as portable and ubiquitous as the paper substrate. The especially well suited instruments for dispensation of conductive inks are rollerball pens as a result of their compatibility with gels and liquids [23]. Commercially available rollerball pens with diameters varying from 250 μm to closely a millimeter are especially fabricated for precision and exact writing on paper [19]. Currently direct writing of functional materials like conductive inks on paper with different type of pens have been made known for fabrication of paper-based point-of-care testing (POCT) platforms [24,25]. For instance, barrier-defined microfluidic channels has been constructed by a commercial pen via direct writing of hydrophobic ink on paper [26]. Also, for writing conductive materials based on graphite as electrodes, commercial pencils have been applied for fabricating components of electrochemical sensing on paper [27–29].

In comparison to the conventional methods in order to fabricate paper-based point-of-care testing platforms, the PoP strategies characterized by useful materials writing on paper through utilizing pens have been broadly applied in the POCT platform for monitoring human health holding to their outstanding benefits of inexpensiveness, quick prototyping abilities, DIY (do-it-yourself), simplicity, accessibility, user-friendliness, hence possessing a significant potential for application in practical usages. Most significantly, these strategies have demonstrated satisfactory possibility in human health controlling applications via monitoring both human body and the ambient environment of human life. PoP strategies have application in manufacturing electrochemical sensors, chemiresistive and piezoresistive sensors. Also, they have employed in fabricating paper-based SERS (surface enhanced Raman scattering) sensors, thanks to their ultra-high detection sensitivity.

The present article describes the development of direct writing of graphite ink in order to fabricate conductive patterns on paper. The synthesized conductive graphite ink flew through the rollerball pen smoothly. The flexibility and conductivity of newly designed patterns on paper were evaluated. Also, for the first time the synthesized graphite nano-ink was used for designing a novel electrochemical immunosensor based on antibody/graphite ink conjugation for detection of CA 15–3 biomarker (carbohydrate antigen 15–3). The main aim of this research is to introduce development of a novel electrochemical bioassay based on combination of anti-CA 15–3 antibody with conductive graphite ink for detection of CA 15–3 biomarker. The antibody mixed graphite ink was synthesized and immobilized on the surface of glassy carbon electrode. Analytical function of immunosensor was evaluated to determine CA 15–3 antigen by means of differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). To the best of acknowledge, this is the first report about detection of CA 15–3 cancer biomarker using the antibody/conductive graphite ink.

All utilized chemicals in this research were purchased from Merck of Germany with analytical reagent grade. Synthetic graphite microcrystals with purity of 99.999% and grade APS of 15–15 μm were prepared from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA). Human plasma samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran). Human CA 15.3 ELISA kit (CanAg /CA15–3 EIA) involving biotinilated solution, dilute solutions, antibody (HRP Labeled anti-CA 15–3) standard, standard buffer, avidin-biotin-peroxidase complex (ABC), CA 15.3 antigen were purchased from CanAg Diagonestic AB Technology Co., (Gotenberg, Sweden). In addition, dilute solutions were prepared daily with precision dilution before use.

Electrochemical measurements were accomplished in a traditional three-electrode cell (from Metrohm) powered by an electrochemical system consisting of PalmSens 4c system, electrochemical interface. The system was run on a laptop using PSTrace5 software. Ag/AgCl-Sat'd KCl (from Metrohm) and platinum wire are used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (d = 2 mm) (from Azar electrode Co., Iran). The electrode surface morphology was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8020, Czech) with an operating voltage of 3 kV, and chemical compositions of the electrode were analyzed by an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) coupled with the FE-SEM equipment. The resistance of conductive nano-ink was evaluated by ohmmeter (XIOLE, XL830L, China, multimeter).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphite ink and CA 15-3 conjugated graphite ink (bioink)
ce:section.ce:para: All solutions used in ink synthesis were prepared with deionized water. In order to synthesis the graphite ink, at first step, polyethylene glycol (PEG) (6 wt% in water) and Chitosan (CS) (0.1 wt% in 0.1 M acetic acid) were mixed together with 2:1 V/V ratio. Next, 2 M glucose solution was prepared and added to the PEG/CS mixture. Then, 0.01 wt% methylene blue (MB) and 3 wt% graphite powder was added to form the ink. Before addition of biotinylated antibody (anti-CA 15–3) to the synthesized graphite ink, the ink was sonicated for 30 min. After sonication, in order to prepare the antibody involved graphite ink, 10 μL of biotinylated antibody (anti-CA 15–3) which was activated with NHS (0.4 M) and EDC (0.1 M) for about 30 min at room temperature (25 °C) was added to the graphite ink (it should be stored in the refrigerator 4 °C). Bare graphite ink (Gr-ink), without biotinylated antibody, was also prepared to compare the performance of the antibody involved graphite ink (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FE-SEM characterization of graphite ink and bioink
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the uniformity of the dispersion of bulk inks and the morphology of surfaces, FE-SEM was employed with 3 kV operating voltage. The FE-SEM images of ink and bioink were presented in Fig. 2 . The graphite particles are closely spaced and create a regular matrix. Chitosan is dissolved in acid (in this work acetic acid), thus forming a regular polymer film. Stretching OH, NH, CO, CH and CO and bending of the NH in the ink mixture creates a uniform and disperse solution [19–22]. Graphite inks were immobilized on the surface of GCE through CC interaction [27–29]. Also, identification of synthesized ink and bioink elements were recognized by Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). In Fig. 3 , the quantitative results validated the existence of carbon elements with high frequency in bulk and GCE modified samples. The large and sharp C peaks are the typical signal for carbon elements in the ink and bioink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of bioink on the surface of GCE
ce:section.ce:para: For preparation of immunosensor, GCE (2 mm in diameter) was polished to a mirror-like finish with 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina slurry (Beuhler, USA) followed by rinsing thoroughly with double distilled water. Next, it was successively sonicated in acetone and double distilled water, and allowed to dry at room temperature. Then, a certain volume (10 μL) of both bare graphite ink and bioink (Ab-graphite ink, anti-CA 15–3/graphite ink) were casted on the surface of GC electrodes using drop-casting and incubated for 6 h at 4 °C and 100% moisture content (for bare graphite incubation time was 2 h at room temperature. Immobilization of ink to the GCE surface is due to the strong bond between the PEG, CS and the electrode surface. The carboxyl group (-COOH) of fc region of antibody (Ab) was activated via NHS/EDC for covalent attachment with GC electrode surface. After incubation of anti-CA 15–3/graphite ink on the surface of GCE, 50 μg/mL of BSA (bovine serum albumin) solution was employed as blocking agent for the free sites of electrode that were not bound to anti-CA 15–3/graphite ink-GCE. The anti-CA 15–3/graphite ink-GCE was immersed in BSA for 30 min at 4 °C. For CA 15–3 sensing, the electrode was incubated with for 2 h at 4 °C with 100% moisture content. It should be noted that after all steps of biological incubation, the obtained electrodes were rinsed with standard washing buffer solution. Scheme 2 depicts all of the immunosensor preparation steps.

After modification of the GCE with ink and bioink, the electrochemical behavior of electrode was investigated before and after incubation with biological elements. According to Fig. 3, CV (cyclic voltammetry), DPV (differential pulse voltammetry), SWV (square wave voltammetry), and ChA (chronoamperometry) techniques were recorded in 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- solution (as supporting electrolyte). For the investigation of immunosensor performance, CVs were conducted in one cycle at a potential range of −1 to +1 V and a sweep rate of 100 mV/s. DVPs and SWVs were also conducted in the potential range of −1 to +1 V. In following, ChAs were carried out at tequilibrium = 2, interval time = 0.1 s, run time = 100 s, and Edc = 0.2 V.

One of the important parameters that affects electroactivity of different types of organic molecules is scan rate. The electrochemical behavior of GCE modified by bioink at different scan rates was evaluated by CV technique (Fig. 6 ). The redox process was adsorption-controlled which obtained from the linear relationship between peak current and square root of scan rate (Fig. 6 C). Two approaches widely employed for studying the reversibility of reactions and determination of whether a reaction is adsorption or diffusion controlled compose of the analysis of dependences: Ln I
p versus Ln v and I
p versus v
1/2 . Fig. 6(B) and (C) demonstrate these plots for the oxidation peak of 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. For reversible or irreversible systems without kinetic complications, I
p varies linearly with v
1/2 , interception the origin. Though, the plot of I
p on v
1/2 exhibited in Fig. 6(C) is linear (R2 = 0.9267), it crosses the origin of axes. In the selected scan rate range from 20 to 200 mV.s−1, oxidation peak currents (I
p ) of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− electrooxidation depends linearly on square root of the scan rate (v
0.5 ) and is described by the following equation:
Ipa / μA = 112.75
v
V / s
0.5
+ 0.2266
R 2
= 0.9267

This dependence does not cross the origin (Fig. 6C). This fact can propose that the electrode process isn't controlled by diffusion and can be preceded by chemical reaction. On the other hand, a dependence of ln I
pa on ln v is linear (Fig. 6B) and described by the following equation:
Ln
Ipa / μA = 0.5426
Ln
v
V / s
+ 4.8334
R 2
= 0.9268
A slope close to 1 is expected for adsorption-controlled electrode processes [30–35]. Its slope is 0.5426 and expressed adsorption-control of the electrode process.

Fig. 6(A) exhibits a dependence of Epa on scan rate determined from CVs recorded for [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− electrooxidation. If electrochemical reaction is irreversible, then Epa is independent on V. Thus, it can be concluded that heterogeneous electron transfers in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− electrooxidation is irreversible because Epa enhances with an increment in the scan rate.

The analytic performance of the engineered immunosensor was studied by DPV technique in different concentrations of CA 15–3 biomarker. For CA 15–3 sensing, GCE/bioink was incubated with various concentrations of CA15–3 antigen. Fig. 7A shows the DPVs results at different concentrations of CA 15–3 biomarkers, also Fig. 7B shows the calibration curve. According to dependency of peak current to the concentration of CA 15–3, linear ranging was from 15 to 250 U/mL which confirm ultra-sensitivity of immunosensor and the proposed method. The linear regression equation obtained is as follows:
I
μA
= 0.0035
C
CA
15 – 3
– 0.1642
R 2
= 0.9698

The calibration plot was then obtained by plotting the peak current against CA15–3 antigen concentration. The dynamic range was found between 15 and 250 U/mL. The lower limits of quantitation (LLOQ) was determined to be 15 U/mL. The appropriate limit of quantification can be considered as a result of several factors involved in the preparation of the desired immunosensor, including excellent electrical conductivity, structural properties and high stability of the Ab/graphite ink matrix, appropriate surface area, and providing the feasibility of biological activity was used to identify the CA 15–3 antigens. These results indicated that GCE/bioink is a suitable platform for detection of CA15–3 in biological fluids.

According to the obtained results, it was possible to apply this technique to the quantitative analysis of CA 15.3 in real samples using DPV technique (Fig. 8 ). As mentioned previously, the 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- of pH 7.4 was selected as the supporting electrolyte for the quantification of CA 15.3. For this purpose, 10 μL of unprocessed human plasma were mixed with 10 μL of different concentrations of CA15-3 antigen. The GCE/bioink was incubated by CA15-3 protein. Using the optimum conditions, DPVs were recorded and calibration curves were obtained. The calibration plot was then obtained by plotting the peak current against the mixture of CA15-3 antigen and plasma concentration. The DPVs (Fig. 8A) show that the developed immunosensor can be detected the biomarker in concentration range of 3.75–250 U/mL, LLOQ was 3.75 U/mL on the human plasma sample and the calibration curve (Fig. 8B) plotted to the linear regression equation is as follows:
I
μA
= 0.0006
C
CA
15 – 3
− 5E − 05
R 2
= 0.9537

Stability and reproducibility are two significant factors that are studied to investigate the performance of proposed biosensor. For this purpose, electrode stability related to the number of cycles was evaluated. The results indicated that with increase of the cycle's numbers to 50, the peak currents increase very smoothly. Results confirmed that the electrode is stable at least 50 replicate analysis. But, after 100 cycle, the peak currents were decrease. Therefore, engineered substrate could be use till 50 time.

In summary, a novel immune-ink was produced and used for the detection of breast cancer biomarker. The provided electrochemical immunosensor based on bioink exhibits appropriate ability to detect the CA 15–3 biomarker in low concentrations in human plasma sample. The electrochemical behavior of engineered immunosensor electrode was evaluated using cyclic voltammetric and differential pulse voltammetry techniques. Under the optimized operating conditions, the low limit of quantitation (LLOQ) for the designed immunosensor was recorded as 15 U/mL, which this evaluation was performed at linear range of 15–250 U/mL. The results were compared with the previous electrochemical immunosensors (Table 2 [36–43]). Obtained results, showed that, utilizing the bioinks could open new strategy for immunoassays and cancer diagnosis. These discussed features of the immune-inks can be a fundamental evolution in the development of any fields of biomedical analysis and especially in clinical diagnosis fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2019.03.170.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CA 15-3: uses and limitation as a biomarker for breast cancer

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M. Hasanzadeh, S. Tagi, E. Solhi, N. Shadjou, A. Jouyban, A. Mokhtarzadeh, Immunosensing of breast cancer prognostic marker in adenocarcinoma cell lysates and unprocessed human plasma samples using gold nanostructure coated on organic substrate, Int. J. Biol. Macromol. 118, 1082–1089.
